sourceSections.title: Eligibility rules
sourceSections.text:
Two tracks, plus the marijuana and OWI specials.

Track A — Set-aside by application (MCL 780.621)
Since the Clean Slate expansion (eff. April 11, 2021), a person may petition the convicting court to set aside:

• Up to three felony convictions in a lifetime (previously only one).
• An unlimited number of misdemeanor convictions.
• Limit: no more than ONE felony conviction for the SAME offense may be set aside if that offense is
punishable by more than 10 years.
The applicant must have completed all sentence terms (incarceration, probation, parole), have no pending
charges, and not be convicted of a new offense during the waiting period.

Track B — Automatic set-aside (MCL 780.621g, eff. April 11, 2023)
The Michigan State Police and courts set aside eligible convictions with NO application:

• Up to two felony convictions, automatically set aside 10 years after sentencing or completion of any term of
imprisonment, whichever is later.
• Up to four misdemeanor convictions (93 days or more), automatically set aside 7 years after sentencing.
• 92-day-or-less misdemeanors are set aside 7 years after sentencing (by the MSP if in its database, otherwise
by the court).
• A person with more than one assaultive conviction does not qualify for automatic set-aside.

Special tracks
• Marijuana (MCL 780.621e): a person convicted of a misdemeanor marihuana offense may apply (form MC
227a) to set it aside; a rebuttable presumption arises that the conduct would not be a crime today, which the
prosecutor must overcome.
• OWI (eff. Feb 2022): a first-violation operating-while-intoxicated offense may be set aside by application 5
years after probation ends (this was newly made eligible; most traffic offenses remain ineligible).
• Human trafficking (MCL 780.621(3)): a victim may apply at any time to set aside an offense that was a
direct result of being trafficked (form MC 227b).

AGENT GUIDANCE
Decide track by timing and limits. If the person wants relief now, or has more convictions than the
automatic caps, use the application track (up to 3 felonies / unlimited misdemeanors). If they qualify and
can wait, automatic set-aside (2 felonies / 4 misdemeanors) needs no filing.
Route marijuana to MCL 780.621e (form MC 227a) and OWI to the 5-year application path — both are
newer carve-ins that users often don't realize exist.

Michigan Set-Aside & Clean Slate — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Waiting periods
sourceSections.text:
Application-track waits run from the LATEST of sentencing, completion of probation, discharge from parole,
or completion of imprisonment. Automatic-track waits run from sentencing (misdemeanors) or
sentencing/release (felonies).

Track / matter Waiting period Measured from

Application — misdemeanor(s) 3 years Latest sentence event

Application — one serious misd., 5 years Latest sentence event
one OWI, or one felony

Application — multiple felonies 7 years Latest sentence event

Automatic — misdemeanor (≥ 93 days 7 years Imposition of sentence
or ≤ 92 days)

Automatic — felony 10 years Sentencing or release, later

Marihuana (MCL 780.621e) None stated On application

Human trafficking victim None Any time after conviction

Two structural rules shape the count. First, the One Bad Night rule (MCL 780.621b): multiple felonies or
multiple misdemeanors are treated as a SINGLE conviction for set-aside purposes if they occurred within 24
hours and arose from the same transaction — UNLESS any of them is an assaultive crime, involves a dangerous
weapon, or carries a penalty of more than 10 years. Second, on the application track the applicant must have no
new conviction during the applicable waiting period and no pending charges; a denied petition generally
cannot be refiled for 3 years (MCL 780.621d(5)). The automatic track simply runs the clock from
sentencing/release and checks eligibility against the database.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Apply the tiers: 3 yrs (misdemeanors), 5 yrs (one serious misdemeanor / one OWI / one felony), 7 yrs
(multiple felonies) for applications; 7 yrs (misdemeanor) and 10 yrs (felony) for automatic.
Use the One Bad Night rule to collapse same-night, same-transaction offenses into one — but check the
assaultive/weapon/10-year exceptions, which break the grouping.

Michigan Set-Aside & Clean Slate — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Disqualifying / ineligible offenses
sourceSections.text:
Some offenses cannot be set aside at all (MCL 780.621c); others are eligible by application but excluded from
AUTOMATIC set-aside.

Never eligible (MCL 780.621c) — neither track
• Felonies (or attempts) punishable by life imprisonment.
• Most serious sex offenses and offenses requiring sex-offender registration; criminal sexual conduct (with a
narrow pre-2015 CSC-4th exception by petition).
• Child sexually abusive activity; certain offenses involving minors.
• Human trafficking (by the offender), terrorism, and other enumerated serious crimes.
• Traffic offenses generally — except a first-violation OWI is now set-aside-eligible by application; most
other traffic offenses, and any causing injury/death, remain ineligible.
• Felony domestic violence if the person has a prior domestic-violence misdemeanor.

Eligible by application but EXCLUDED from automatic set-aside
• Assaultive crimes (and a person with more than one assaultive conviction is barred from automatic relief
entirely).
• Serious misdemeanors (as defined by statute).
• Crimes of dishonesty.
• Offenses punishable by 10 or more years.
• Any offense involving a minor, a vulnerable adult, injury or serious impairment, or death.

Other limits
• Application caps: no more than three felonies total, and only one felony for the same >10-year offense.
• Automatic caps: no more than two felonies and four (≥93-day) misdemeanors.
• New conviction / pending charge during the wait defeats an application; refiling after denial is barred for 3
years.
• Out-of-state and federal convictions cannot be set aside under Michigan law.

VERIFY BEFORE RELYING
The never-eligible list (MCL 780.621c) and the automatic-track exclusions (assaultive, serious
misdemeanor, dishonesty, 10-year, minor/vulnerable-adult/injury/death) are defined by specific
statutes and were amended repeatedly in the Clean Slate package and afterward. The agent must
map each offense to its MCL section and apply the correct track's exclusion list — an offense can
be eligible by application yet excluded from automatic relief.

Michigan Set-Aside & Clean Slate — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

AGENT GUIDANCE
Run two screens: first, is the offense never eligible (life felony, most sex offenses, most traffic)? Then, if
seeking automatic relief, is it on the automatic-exclusion list (assaultive, serious misdemeanor,
dishonesty, 10-year, minor/vulnerable/injury/death)?
Remember the asymmetry: assaultive and dishonesty crimes can often still be set aside BY
APPLICATION even though they're excluded from automatic relief.

Michigan Set-Aside & Clean Slate — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Case outcome rules
sourceSections.text:
Conviction type and track drive the timing and whether a filing is needed. This table is the agent's primary
routing logic for Michigan.

Conviction / matter Track Route Wait / limit

Misdemeanor(s) Application Petition (MC 227) 3 yrs; unlimited number

One serious misd. / one OWI / Application Petition (MC 227) 5 yrs
one felony

Multiple felonies Application Petition (MC 227) 7 yrs; ≤ 3 felonies

Misdemeanor (eligible) Automatic No filing 7 yrs; ≤ 4

Felony (eligible) Automatic No filing 10 yrs; ≤ 2

Misdemeanor marihuana Application Petition (MC 227a) Rebuttable presumption

Human trafficking victim Application Petition (MC 227b) Any time

Life felony / most sex / most — Not eligible Categorical
traffic / assaultive×2 (auto)

Effect of relief: a set-aside makes the conviction NONPUBLIC — it no longer appears on a public background
check and the person may generally treat it as not having occurred for most purposes. But the Michigan State
Police retain a NONPUBLIC record (MCL 780.623) accessible to law enforcement, courts, and for certain
licensing and employment screening, and a set-aside does NOT automatically restore firearm rights (gun-rights
restoration is a separate analysis under MCL 750.224f / 28.424). The relief reaches Michigan records only.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Tell users a set-aside makes the record nonpublic but not erased — MSP keeps a nonpublic copy for law
enforcement and certain licensing, and it does not by itself restore gun rights.
If a record is automatic-eligible, the person may not need to file at all — have them check their MSP
record (Clean Slate) before preparing an application.

Michigan Set-Aside & Clean Slate — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Required forms
sourceSections.text:
The application track uses State Court Administrative Office (SCAO) forms filed in the convicting court; the
automatic track needs no form. Pick the form by offense type.

Application-track forms
• MC 227 — Application to Set Aside Conviction(s): the main form for non-marijuana convictions, with
checkboxes for the 3-year (misdemeanors), 5-year (one serious misdemeanor / one OWI / one felony), and
7-year (multiple felonies) paths. Requires a certified copy of each conviction.
• MC 227a — Application to Set Aside Marihuana Conviction(s): for misdemeanor marihuana offenses
under MCL 780.621e (rebuttable presumption).
• MC 227b — human-trafficking victim: for an offense that was a direct result of being trafficked (MCL
780.621(3)).
• An Order (set-aside / denial) accompanies the application for the judge's signature.

Supporting steps
• Fingerprints to the Michigan State Police with the application fee, so the MSP can verify the record and
process the set-aside.
• Certified copy of each conviction from the convicting court, attached to the application.
• Service on the prosecuting attorney and the Michigan Attorney General.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Use MC 227 for ordinary convictions, MC 227a for misdemeanor marijuana, MC 227b for trafficking
victims; attach a certified copy of each conviction and submit fingerprints with the fee to the MSP.
Where relief is automatic, there is nothing to file — direct the user to verify via their MSP Clean Slate
record rather than preparing an MC 227.

Michigan Set-Aside & Clean Slate — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Filing instructions
sourceSections.text:
1 Get the record. Obtain your Michigan State Police criminal history (ICHAT) and the court disposition to
confirm each conviction, its class, sentence-completion dates, and whether automatic set-aside has already
applied.

2 Choose the track and confirm eligibility. Application vs. automatic; the waiting period; the One Bad Night
grouping; the application caps (≤ 3 felonies) or automatic caps (≤ 2 felonies / 4 misdemeanors); and the
exclusion lists.

3 Complete the form (MC 227, MC 227a, or MC 227b) and attach a certified copy of each conviction; sign
before a clerk or notary.

4 File in the convicting court and submit fingerprints with the fee to the Michigan State Police (the
application processing fee is set by statute, commonly around $50).
5 Serve the prosecuting attorney and the Michigan Attorney General; either may object.
6 Hearing. The court typically holds a hearing (for marijuana, a hearing follows if the prosecutor contests the
presumption); the judge weighs the circumstances and the public interest.

7 Order and processing. If granted, the court enters the set-aside Order and forwards it to the Michigan State
Police, which makes the conviction nonpublic while retaining the MCL 780.623 nonpublic record. Keep a
certified copy of the order.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Venue is the convicting court — file where each conviction was entered; convictions in different courts
need separate applications.
The fingerprint + MSP step and service on both the prosecutor and the Attorney General are easy to
miss; build them into the checklist.

Michigan Set-Aside & Clean Slate — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Court-specific quirks
sourceSections.text:
• Two tracks, different limits. Application (≤ 3 felonies / unlimited misdemeanors) vs. automatic (≤ 2
felonies / 4 misdemeanors). They run in parallel — a person can file an application for convictions the
automatic system won't reach.
• One Bad Night (MCL 780.621b). Same-transaction offenses within 24 hours count as a single conviction —
unless assaultive, weapon-involved, or >10-year, which break the grouping.
• Assaultive/dishonesty asymmetry. These can often be set aside by application but are excluded from
automatic relief; more than one assaultive conviction bars automatic relief entirely.
• OWI carve-in (Feb 2022). A first-offense OWI became set-aside-eligible by application 5 years after
probation ends — a recent and frequently unknown change.
• Marijuana presumption (MCL 780.621e). A misdemeanor marijuana set-aside carries a rebuttable
presumption the conduct wouldn't be a crime today; the prosecutor bears the burden to rebut.
• MSP keeps a nonpublic record. Set-aside makes the conviction nonpublic, not destroyed; law enforcement
and certain licensing still see it (MCL 780.623).
• Firearms not auto-restored. A set-aside does not by itself restore gun rights; restoration is a separate
process (MCL 750.224f / 28.424).
• 3-year refiling bar. A denied application generally cannot be refiled for 3 years unless the court allows
earlier.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Lead with the MSP Clean Slate check: many users are already (or soon will be) automatically cleared
and don't need to file.
Surface the OWI and marijuana carve-ins — they're recent and users rarely know a first OWI or an old
marijuana misdemeanor can now be cleared.

Michigan Set-Aside & Clean Slate — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.title: Fee information
sourceSections.text:
Item Amount Note

Application processing fee (MSP) ~$50 Statutory fee with the fingerprint submission

Automatic set-aside $0 No application, no fee

Marihuana set-aside (MC 227a) Application fee Rebuttable-presumption process

Certified copy of conviction Court fee One per conviction, attached to the application

Michigan State Police ICHAT record ICHAT fee To confirm record / automatic status

Attorney fees (if represented) Varies Not required; legal-aid and county expungement clinics assist

Michigan is relatively low-cost. Automatic set-aside is free. The application track carries a statutory Michigan
State Police processing fee (commonly around $50) submitted with the fingerprints, plus the cost of a certified
copy of each conviction and any ICHAT record check. Many counties and nonprofits run free expungement
clinics (e.g., through Michigan Works! and county conviction-integrity units) that help with the forms and fees.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Quote the ~$50 MSP processing fee for applications and note automatic set-aside is free; point users to
county expungement clinics for free help with the forms.
Have users pull their ICHAT record first — it confirms eligibility and whether automatic relief has already
cleared the conviction, avoiding an unnecessary fee.

Michigan Set-Aside & Clean Slate — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase

sourceSections.text:
An eligible felony is set aside automatically 10 years after
sentencing/release; up to FOUR eligible (>=93-day) misdemeanors

A misdemeanor marihuana conviction may be set aside by

would not be a crime today, and the prosecutor must rebut it.

Order on Application
The MC 227 application is paired with an Order. If granted, the Order sets aside the conviction(s) and directs
the Michigan State Police to make the conviction nonpublic while retaining the nonpublic record under MCL
